paper_title: Sediment Analysis and Age Modeling at Ossipee Lake

methods.description: Process for conducting fieldwork at Ossipee Lake, performing subbottom sonar surveys, and collecting various types of sediment cores.

steps.description: Carried out fieldwork at Ossipee Lake during June 2017 and June 2018.
steps.searchable_summary: Conduct fieldwork at specified location and dates

steps.description: Subbottom sonar surveys were conducted using a SyQuest StrataBox HD single-frequency (10 kHz) instrument.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform subbottom sonar survey using specified equipment

steps.description: Subbottom depths were approximated based on the known water depth and assumption of a constant speed of sound.
steps.searchable_summary: Approximate subbottom depths from sonar data using water depth and sound speed

steps.description: Sediment cores were collected from both the northern (43°48.12 ′ N, 71°9.00 ′ W; 19.5 m water depth) and southern (43°47.16 ′ N, 71°8.28 ′ W; 17.7 m water depth) ends of the large central basin of the lake (Fig. 3).
steps.searchable_summary: Collect sediment cores from specified geographic coordinates and water depths
inputs: location: Ossipee Lake northern site (43°48.12 ′ N, 71°9.00 ′ W, 19.5 m depth)
location: Ossipee Lake southern site (43°47.16 ′ N, 71°8.28 ′ W, 17.7 m depth)

steps.description: Short (∼1 m) gravity cores were collected to ensure an intact sediment-water interface; transparent core tubes allowed field verification of undisturbed sediment overlain by clear water.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect short gravity cores (~1m) using transparent tubes
outputs: short gravity cores with intact sediment-water interface

steps.description: A single ∼2 m piston-percussion core was collected at the northern site.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect a ~2m piston-percussion core at the northern site

steps.description: Two overlapping sequences of multiple 3 m drives were collected from the southern site using a Uwitec pistonpercussion corer.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect overlapping sequences of multiple 3m piston-percussion core drives at the southern site using Uwitec corer
outputs: overlapping sequences of piston-percussion core drives

methods.method_name: Sediment Core Processing and Initial Laboratory Analysis
methods.description: Laboratory procedures for splitting, describing, photographing, and performing initial analyses (X-radiography, XRF, magnetic susceptibility, LOI, dry bulk density) on collected sediment cores.

steps.description: Sediment cores were split lengthwise, visually described, and photographed before further analyses were conducted.
steps.searchable_summary: Split sediment cores lengthwise, visually describe, and photograph

steps.description: We used an ITRAX (Cox Analytical Systems; Croudace et al., 2006) core scanner housed in the Geosciences Department at the University of Massachusetts Amherst to obtain X-radiographs and measure bulk elemental abundances via scanning X-ray fluorescence (XRF) using a Mo X-ray source at 30 kV voltage and 55 mA current.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform X-radiography and scanning XRF on split cores using ITRAX scanner

steps.description: Cores were scanned at 1.0 mm resolution with a count time of 10 s, except for the short surface cores, which were scanned using a 15 s count time.
steps.searchable_summary: Configure ITRAX scanning resolution and count time

steps.description: We limit our reporting of XRF results to a subset of elements that provide insight on terrestrial sediment input. Specifically, we report results for potassium, which has been shown to be a reliable indicator of clastic sediment input to northeastern lakes... in addition to silicon and titanium.
steps.searchable_summary: Focus XRF data analysis on specific elements (K, Si, Ti) relevant to terrestrial input

steps.description: Magnetic susceptibility was measured using a Bartington MS2E sensor at 0.5 cm intervals using split-core logging methodology (Nowaczyk, 2001).
steps.searchable_summary: Measure magnetic susceptibility on split cores at 0.5 cm intervals using Bartington MS2E sensor

steps.description: Percent loss on ignition (LOI) and dry bulk density (ρ db ) were measured on 1 cm 3 subsamples removed from the cores at 1 cm intervals following standard procedures (Dean, 1974).
steps.searchable_summary: Remove 1 cm^3 subsamples at 1 cm intervals and measure LOI and dry bulk density

methods.description: Applying multiple dating techniques including radiocarbon, 137Cs, and 210Pb stratigraphy to establish age control points for the sediment sequence.

steps.description: Age constraints for the sedimentary sequence were obtained from a combination of 137 Cs and 210 Pb stratigraphy and radiocarbon dating of nine terrestrial macrofossils and two bulk sediment samples recovered from the cores ( Table 1).
steps.searchable_summary: Identify and collect samples for radiocarbon dating (macrofossils, bulk sediment)
outputs: radiocarbon dating samples (9 macrofossils, 2 bulk sediment)

steps.description: Radiocarbon dates were determined via accelerator mass spectrometry (AMS) at the National Ocean Sciences Accelerator Mass Spectrometry laboratory at Woods Hole Oceanographic Institute in Woods Hole, Massachusetts, and at Direct AMS in Bothell, Washington.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform radiocarbon dating using Accelerator Mass Spectrometry (AMS)

steps.description: Radiocarbon ages were calibrated to calendar years BP, where present corresponds to 1950 CE, utilizing the IntCal13 calibration curve (Reimer et al., 2013) and the open-source R package CLAM (Blaauw, 2010).
steps.searchable_summary: Calibrate raw radiocarbon ages to calendar years BP using IntCal13 curve and R package CLAM

steps.description: One-centimeter-thick slices of sediment were dried, homogenized, and analyzed via gamma spectroscopy using a Canberra GL2020 R low-energy gamma detector to determine 137 Cs and 210 Pb activity profiles for the upper 35 cm of the southern core sequence.
steps.searchable_summary: Slice, dry, homogenize sediment and analyze via gamma spectroscopy for 137Cs and 210Pb profiles
inputs: equipment: Canberra GL2020 R gamma detector

steps.description: The onset (1954 CE) of detectable 137 Cs and subsequent peak (1963 CE) provided two additional age-control points used in the final construction of an age-depth model for the composite sequence.
steps.searchable_summary: Identify 137Cs onset and peak dates as age control points

steps.description: In addition, the open-source R package serac (Bruel and Sabatier, 2020) was used to evaluate the 210 Pb profile using both the constant rate of supply (CRS; Appleby and Oldfield, 1978) and constant flux constant sedimentation (CFCS; Krishnaswamy et al., 1971) models.
steps.searchable_summary: Evaluate 210Pb profile using CRS and CFCS models with R package serac

steps.description: Ultimately, the sedimentation rate determined via application of the CFCS model was used to estimate the age (1890 ±10 yr) of the first prominent stratigraphic horizon that could be correlated among all cores (at 33.5 cm depth in the southern composite sequence), providing one additional control point for the age-depth model.
steps.searchable_summary: Estimate age of a stratigraphic horizon using CFCS model sedimentation rate

methods.description: Constructing a final age-depth model for the southern composite sediment sequence using a combination of radiocarbon and radioisotope age control points.

steps.description: We used the three control points established from the 137 Cs and 210 Pb stratigraphies along with 10 radiocarbon dates to construct a final age-depth model for the southern composite sequence.
steps.searchable_summary: Combine age control points from 137Cs, 210Pb, and radiocarbon dating

steps.description: A variety of age-depth modeling methods were investigated, including various interpolation methods using "classical" techniques (Blaauw, 2010), Bayesian methods (Bacon; Blaauw and Christen, 2011), and incorporation of time-varying accumulation rates derived from variations in sediment composition (Minderhoud et al., 2016).
steps.searchable_summary: Investigate different age-depth modeling methods (interpolation, Bayesian, time-varying accumulation)

steps.description: As described herein, we utilized the age-depth model derived from linear interpolation between control points defined by the 2-sigma uncertainty in the calibrated ages calculated in the R package CLAM (Blaauw, 2010).
steps.searchable_summary: Construct age-depth model using linear interpolation between control points with 2-sigma uncertainty (R package CLAM)

steps.description: Note the following caveats. Linear interpolation in CLAM underestimates the uncertainty in the age of sediment at most depths, produces (potentially spurious) inflection points in accumulation rate at each control point, and is unable to account for changes in sedimentation rate between control points, resulting in underestimation of peak accumulation rates during short-duration events.
steps.searchable_summary: Document and consider limitations/caveats of the chosen linear interpolation age-depth model

methods.method_name: Clastic Mass Accumulation Rate and Suspended Sediment Yield Calculation
methods.description: Calculating proxies for watershed sediment input (MAR clastic and SSY) from sediment core data and lake/catchment characteristics.

steps.description: MAR clastic per unit area of lake bottom (g/cm 2 /yr) was determined using the equation: MAR clastic = SR * ρ db * (1 - (LOI / 100)) where SR is the instantaneous bulk sedimentation rate (cm/yr) defined by the slope of the age-depth model and ρ db and LOI are the dry bulk density (g/cm 3 ) and percent mass loss on ignition, respectively, at corresponding depths.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate clastic mass accumulation rate (MAR clastic) using sedimentation rate, dry bulk density, and LOI

steps.description: Rather than using the total surface area of the lake, we based LA on the area of the lake below the 8 m depth contour (7.5 km 2 ), the area within which the majority of Holocene sediment infill is observed based on interpretation of the subbottom sonar transects (Fig. 4).
steps.searchable_summary: Define effective lake area (LA) based on area below 8m depth contour

steps.description: Estimates of SSY in Mg/yr/km 2 were derived from MAR clastic using the equation: SSY = MAR clastic * (LA / CA) where LA and CA are the lake area and catchment area, respectively.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate suspended sediment yield (SSY) using MAR clastic, effective lake area (LA), and catchment area (CA)
